---
title: "Examples gallery"
jupyter: python3
---

A worked **basic** (minimal call) and **advanced** (full options) example for every
analytical function in expdpy, each run live on the bundled `kuznets` country–year
panel so you can see the actual output. This mirrors the
[Function reference](reference/index.qmd) one entry at a time; for a narrative tour
of a typical workflow, see the [Quickstart](quickstart.qmd) instead.

Every example assumes the following setup:

```{python}
import numpy as np
import expdpy as ex
from expdpy.data import load_kuznets

df = load_kuznets()
```

## Tables

### `prepare_descriptive_table`

N, mean, standard deviation and quantiles for the numeric variables.

Basic:

```{python}
ex.prepare_descriptive_table(df).gt
```

Advanced — set the decimals per statistic (`None` drops that column) and a caption:

```{python}
ex.prepare_descriptive_table(
    df,
    digits=(0, 2, 2, None, None, 2, None, None),
    caption="Kuznets panel",
).gt
```

### `prepare_correlation_table`

Pearson correlations above the diagonal, Spearman below; significant cells in bold.

Basic:

```{python}
ex.prepare_correlation_table(df[["gini_regional", "gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc"]]).gt
```

Advanced — more decimals, a stricter bold threshold, and a caption:

```{python}
ex.prepare_correlation_table(
    df[["gini_regional", "gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc", "trade_share"]],
    digits=3,
    bold=0.01,
    caption="Correlations (kuznets)",
).gt
```

### `prepare_ext_obs_table`

The top and bottom `n` observations of a variable.

Basic:

```{python}
ex.prepare_ext_obs_table(df, n=5).gt
```

Advanced — the ten most extreme observations of a chosen variable, showing only the
panel identifiers and that variable:

```{python}
ex.prepare_ext_obs_table(
    df, n=10, cs_id=["country"], ts_id="year", var="gini_regional"
).gt
```

## Graphs

### `prepare_correlation_graph`

Basic — a correlation heatmap:

```{python}
ex.prepare_correlation_graph(df[["gini_regional", "gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc"]]).fig
```

Advanced — the ellipse style (R `corrplot` look):

```{python}
ex.prepare_correlation_graph(
    df[["gini_regional", "gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc", "trade_share"]],
    style="ellipse",
).fig
```

### `prepare_trend_graph`

Basic — the mean of one variable over time, with standard-error bars:

```{python}
ex.prepare_trend_graph(df, ts_id="year", var=["gini_regional"]).fig
```

Advanced — several variables on one chart:

```{python}
ex.prepare_trend_graph(df, ts_id="year", var=["gini_regional", "trade_share"]).fig
```

### `prepare_quantile_trend_graph`

Basic — the default quantiles of a variable over time:

```{python}
ex.prepare_quantile_trend_graph(df, ts_id="year", var="gini_regional").fig
```

Advanced — custom quantile levels and no per-observation points:

```{python}
ex.prepare_quantile_trend_graph(
    df, ts_id="year", var="gini_regional", quantiles=(0.1, 0.5, 0.9), points=False
).fig
```

### `prepare_by_group_bar_graph`

Basic — the group mean of a variable:

```{python}
ex.prepare_by_group_bar_graph(df, "continent", "gini_regional").fig
```

Advanced — a different statistic, bars ordered by it, and a custom color:

```{python}
ex.prepare_by_group_bar_graph(
    df, "continent", "gini_regional",
    stat_fun=np.nanmedian, order_by_stat=True, color="#4682b4",
).fig
```

### `prepare_by_group_trend_graph`

Basic — one line per group over time:

```{python}
ex.prepare_by_group_trend_graph(
    df, ts_id="year", group_var="continent", var="gini_regional"
).fig
```

Advanced — add standard-error bars and drop the markers:

```{python}
ex.prepare_by_group_trend_graph(
    df, ts_id="year", group_var="continent", var="gini_regional",
    error_bars=True, points=False,
).fig
```

### `prepare_by_group_violin_graph`

This function returns a Plotly figure directly (no `.fig`).

Basic — the distribution of a variable across groups:

```{python}
ex.prepare_by_group_violin_graph(df, "continent", "gini_regional")
```

Advanced — order groups by mean and orient the violins vertically:

```{python}
ex.prepare_by_group_violin_graph(
    df, "continent", "gini_regional", order_by_mean=True, group_on_y=False
)
```

### `prepare_histogram`

Basic — a 30-bin histogram:

```{python}
ex.prepare_histogram(df, "gini_regional").fig
```

Advanced — finer bins on another variable:

```{python}
ex.prepare_histogram(df, "gdp_pc", bins=50).fig
```

### `prepare_bar_chart`

Basic — category counts:

```{python}
ex.prepare_bar_chart(df, "continent").fig
```

Advanced — order bars by descending count and set a custom color:

```{python}
ex.prepare_bar_chart(df, "continent", order_by_count=True, color="red").fig
```

### `prepare_missing_values_graph`

This function returns a Plotly figure directly (no `.fig`).

Basic — the fraction of missing values by variable and year:

```{python}
ex.prepare_missing_values_graph(df, ts_id="year")
```

Advanced — restrict to numeric variables and show only whether values are missing:

```{python}
ex.prepare_missing_values_graph(df, ts_id="year", no_factors=True, binary=True)
```

### `prepare_scatter_plot`

This function returns a Plotly figure directly (no `.fig`).

Basic — a plain scatter of two variables:

```{python}
ex.prepare_scatter_plot(df, x="log_gdp_pc", y="gini_regional")
```

Advanced — map color and size to other columns and add a size-weighted LOESS
smoother (the N-shaped Kuznets curve):

```{python}
ex.prepare_scatter_plot(
    df, x="log_gdp_pc", y="gini_regional",
    color="continent", size="population", loess=2, alpha=0.6,
)
```

## Regression

### `prepare_regression_table`

Basic — a pooled OLS regression of the cubic Kuznets curve:

```{python}
ex.prepare_regression_table(
    df,
    dvs="gini_regional",
    idvs=["log_gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc_sq", "log_gdp_pc_cu"],
).etable
```

Advanced — absorb two-way (country + year) fixed effects with standard errors
clustered by country:

```{python}
ex.prepare_regression_table(
    df,
    dvs="gini_regional",
    idvs=["log_gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc_sq", "log_gdp_pc_cu"],
    feffects=["country", "year"],
    clusters=["country"],
).etable
```

### `prepare_fwl_plot`

Basic — the partial relationship of the outcome and a single regressor:

```{python}
ex.prepare_fwl_plot(df, dv="gini_regional", var="log_gdp_pc").fig
```

Advanced — residualize on the other cubic terms and two-way fixed effects, with the
reported standard error clustered by country:

```{python}
ex.prepare_fwl_plot(
    df,
    dv="gini_regional",
    var="log_gdp_pc",
    controls=["log_gdp_pc_sq", "log_gdp_pc_cu"],
    feffects=["country", "year"],
    clusters=["country"],
).fig
```

## Estimation

### `prepare_estimation`

A unified estimator: OLS by default, or IV/2SLS, Poisson and logit/probit via `model=`,
with a wide choice of standard errors and stepwise model comparison.

Basic — a cumulative-stepwise OLS comparison (watch the estimate move as terms are added):

```{python}
ex.prepare_estimation(
    df,
    dv="gini_regional",
    idvs=["log_gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc_sq", "log_gdp_pc_cu"],
    stepwise="csw",
).etable
```

Advanced — instrumental variables (2SLS) with clustered standard errors, then a
plain-language reading:

```{python}
res = ex.prepare_estimation(
    df,
    dv="gini_regional",
    idvs=["log_gdp_pc_sq"],
    model="iv",
    endog=["log_gdp_pc"],
    instruments=["log_gdp_pc_cu"],
    cluster=["country"],
)
print(res.interpret())
res.etable
```

### `prepare_coefficient_plot`

Coefficient estimates with confidence intervals; pass a fitted result or several models.

Basic:

```{python}
reg = ex.prepare_regression_table(
    df, dvs="gini_regional", idvs=["log_gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc_sq", "log_gdp_pc_cu"]
)
ex.prepare_coefficient_plot(reg).fig
```

Advanced — compare pooled OLS and two-way fixed effects side by side:

```{python}
pooled = ex.prepare_regression_table(df, dvs="gini_regional", idvs=["log_gdp_pc"])
fe = ex.prepare_regression_table(
    df, dvs="gini_regional", idvs=["log_gdp_pc"], feffects=["country", "year"]
)
ex.prepare_coefficient_plot(
    [pooled.models[0], fe.models[0]],
    model_labels=["Pooled OLS", "Two-way FE"],
    keep=["log_gdp_pc"],
).fig
```

## Post-estimation

### `prepare_fixef_plot`

The estimated fixed effects of a model, ranked.

```{python}
country_fe = ex.prepare_regression_table(
    df, dvs="gini_regional", idvs=["log_gdp_pc"], feffects=["country", "year"]
)
ex.prepare_fixef_plot(country_fe, fixef="country", top_n=20).fig
```

### `prepare_predictions`

Fitted values, with residuals and actuals in-sample.

```{python}
ex.prepare_predictions(reg).df.head()
```

### `prepare_joint_test`

A Wald joint-significance test that several coefficients are all zero.

```{python}
joint = ex.prepare_joint_test(reg, ["log_gdp_pc", "log_gdp_pc_sq", "log_gdp_pc_cu"])
print(joint.summary())
```

## Robust inference

### `prepare_robust_inference`

Randomization inference for one coefficient (the wild cluster bootstrap is also available
via `method="wildboot"` when the optional `wildboottest` package is installed).

```{python}
clustered = ex.prepare_regression_table(
    df, dvs="gini_regional", idvs=["log_gdp_pc"], feffects=["country"], clusters=["country"]
)
ri = ex.prepare_robust_inference(clustered, "log_gdp_pc", method="ritest", reps=200, seed=0)
ri.p_value, ri.conf_int
```

## Event study & difference-in-differences

These use the bundled staggered difference-in-differences panel:

```{python}
from expdpy.data import load_staggered_did

did = load_staggered_did()
```

### `prepare_panel_view`

Visualize the treatment structure — which units are treated, and when.

```{python}
ex.prepare_panel_view(did, unit="unit", time="year", cohort="cohort").fig
```

### `prepare_event_study`

Dynamic treatment effects with a built-in pre-trend diagnostic. Basic — Gardner's `did2s`:

```{python}
es = ex.prepare_event_study(
    did, outcome="outcome", unit="unit", time="year", cohort="cohort", estimator="did2s"
)
print(es.interpret())
es.fig
```

Advanced — the Sun–Abraham (`saturated`) estimator, one curve per treatment cohort:

```{python}
ex.prepare_event_study(
    did, outcome="outcome", unit="unit", time="year", cohort="cohort", estimator="saturated"
).fig
```

## Panel models

These require the optional `panel` extra (`pip install "expdpy[panel]"`, via linearmodels),
so the snippets are shown but not executed here.

### `prepare_panel_table`

Pooled / between / fixed-effects / random-effects estimates side by side.

```{python}
#| eval: false
ex.prepare_panel_table(
    did, dv="outcome", idvs=["treated"], entity="unit", time="year"
).etable
```

### `prepare_hausman_test`

Choose between fixed and random effects.

```{python}
#| eval: false
print(
    ex.prepare_hausman_test(
        did, dv="outcome", idvs=["treated"], entity="unit", time="year"
    ).interpret()
)
```

## Concept sandboxes

Simulated demonstrations — each returns a figure, a small comparison table and a `summary`
of the facts it turns on. Turn the knobs to see the concept change.

### `sandbox_omitted_variable_bias`

```{python}
ex.sandbox_omitted_variable_bias(corr_xz=0.6).fig
```

### `sandbox_pooled_vs_fixed_effects`

```{python}
ex.sandbox_pooled_vs_fixed_effects(unit_effect_corr=0.8).fig
```

### `sandbox_clustering_se`

```{python}
ex.sandbox_clustering_se(icc=0.4).fig
```

## Pedagogy

### `explain`

A concept explainer for any method; `ex.list_topics()` lists them all.

```{python}
print(ex.explain("fixed_effects"))
```

## Data preparation

### `treat_outliers`

Basic — winsorize a single variable at the 1st/99th percentile:

```{python}
ex.treat_outliers(df["gdp_pc"], percentile=0.01).describe()
```

Advanced — winsorize several columns at the 5th/95th percentile, with cut-offs
computed within each continent:

```{python}
ex.treat_outliers(
    df[["gini_regional", "gdp_pc"]], percentile=0.05, by=df["continent"]
).describe()
```
